sn45.4:1.2: Then Ven. Ānanda early in the morning adjusted his under robe and—carrying his bowl & outer robes—went into Sāvatthī for alms.
sn45.4:1.3: He saw the brahman Jāṇussoṇin leaving Sāvatthī in an all-white chariot drawn by mares.
sn45.4:1.4: White were the yoked horses, white the ornaments, white the chariot, white the upholstery, white the reins, white the goad, white the canopy, white his turban, white his clothes, white his sandals, and with a white yak-tail fan he was fanned.
sn45.4:2.1: Then Ven. Ānanda, having gone for alms in Sāvatthī, after the meal, returning from his alms round, went to the Blessed One and, on arrival, having bowed down to him, sat to one side. As he was sitting there, he said to the Blessed One,
sn45.4:3.1: “Just now, lord, early in the morning, I adjusted my under robe and—carrying my bowl & outer robes—went into Sāvatthī for alms.
sn45.4:3.2: I saw the brahman Jāṇussoṇin leaving Sāvatthī in an all-white chariot drawn by mares.
sn45.4:3.3: White were the yoked horses, white the ornaments, white the chariot, white the upholstery, white the reins, white the goad, white the canopy, white his turban, white his clothes, white his sandals, and with a white yak-tail fan he was fanned.
sn45.4:3.7: Is it possible to designate a sublime vehicle in this Dhamma-Vinaya?”
sn45.4:4.1: “It is possible, Ānanda,” said the Blessed One.
sn45.4:4.2: “That is a synonym for this very same noble eightfold path:
sn45.4:5.1: “Right view, Ānanda, when developed & pursued, has the subduing of passion as its end-point, the subduing of aversion as its end-point, the subduing of delusion as its end-point.
sn45.4:5.8: Right concentration, when developed & pursued, has the subduing of passion as its end-point, the subduing of aversion as its end-point, the subduing of delusion as its end-point.
sn45.4:6.1: “It is by this sequence of reasons that one can know how that is a synonym for this very same noble eightfold path:
sn45.4:6.3: That is what the Blessed One said.
sn45.4:7.1: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
sn45.4:8.4: shame its pole, the heart its yoke-tie,
sn45.4:9.3: jhāna the axle, persistence the wheels,
sn45.4:9.4: equanimity the balance of the yoke,
sn45.4:10.2: non-ill will, harmlessness, & seclusion its weapons,
sn45.4:10.4: It rolls to security from bondage.
sn45.4:11.1: Coming into play from within oneself:
sn45.4:11.3: They, the enlightened, leave the world.
